Tonal's D7no5/C is the same musical analysis with explicit fifth omission. WhatChord does not currently display omission notation, and the missing fifth is conventional in dominant-seventh shell voicings.

0-2-6_b2.label: clearly-correct
0-2-6_b2.note: D7 is correct because D-F♯-C supplies the complete dominant shell: root, major third, and flat seventh. Tonal's D7no5 is more explicit about the omitted fifth, but musicians commonly write this voicing as D7 when the fifth is absent.

0-2-6_b6.label: clearly-correct
0-2-6_b6.note: D7/F♯ is correct because F♯ is the third in the bass of the D dominant-seventh shell D-F♯-C. Tonal's D7no5/F# is the same inversion with explicit omission notation; WhatChord's shorter label is musically acceptable because omitted fifths are standard in dominant seventh chords.

0-2-6-8_b2.label: clearly-correct
0-2-6-8_b2.note: D7♭5 is correct because D is the bass/root and the voicing supplies the complete D-F♯-A♭-C dominant-flat-five shell. Tonal agrees and lists the tritone-related A♭/G♯7♭5/D reading second. Music21 chooses that tritone twin, which is pitch-class valid, but it makes the observed D bass the flat fifth rather than naming the root-position dominant most musicians would expect.

0-2-7_b2.label: oracle-limitation
0-2-7_b2.note: D7sus4 is preferred because D is the bass/root and C is the flat seventh, giving a conventional sparse dominant-sus shorthand even though the fifth is omitted. Music21 agrees semantically as Dsus add C omit A. Tonal's Csus2/D and Gsus4/D are valid pitch-class rotations, and WhatChord surfaces Gsus4/D as a possible alternate, but the D bass makes the dominant-sus label the clearest context-free primary.

0-2-8_b0.label: clearly-correct
0-2-8_b0.note: A♭♯11/C is preferred because the voicing presents A♭-C as a readable major-third sonority with D as Lydian sharp-eleventh color and C as the third in the bass. Tonal's A♭(♭5)/C is pitch-class equivalent, but the theoretically exact flat-five spelling becomes G♯(♭5)/B♯ in WhatChord's role-aware spelling, which is less readable for this slash voicing. The flat-five interpretation remains surfaced as an alternative.

0-2-9_b0.label: context-dependent
0-2-9_b0.note: Amadd11/C is defensible because A-C-D supplies a clear A minor-third sonority with D as added-eleventh color and C as the minor third in the bass. Music21's Dpower/C names the alternate D-A no-third fifth with C as flat-seventh color, which may be preferable in a context that establishes D as root. WhatChord does not currently emit no-third/power-chord vocabulary, and promoting the incomplete D7/C candidate would imply a missing third rather than naming the sparse sonority directly.

0-2-9_b2.label: context-dependent
0-2-9_b2.note: Am/D is a practical upper-structure spelling for A-C-D: A-C supplies the minor-third sonority while D sits in the bass as add-eleventh slash color. Music21's DpoweraddC reflects the competing D-rooted no-third fifth with C as flat-seventh color. The D bass makes that reading contextually plausible, but without D5(add♭7) or D7(no3) vocabulary the surfaced Am/D label is the clearer available chord symbol.

0-2-9_b9.label: context-dependent
0-2-9_b9.note: Amadd11 is defensible in root position because A-C is a direct minor-third sonority and D is conventional added-eleventh color. Music21's Dpower/AaddC instead hears D-A as a no-third fifth with A in the bass and C as flat-seventh color. That alternate is valid when D is established by context, but WhatChord's A-rooted label is the more complete context-free name within the current vocabulary.

0-3-8_b0.label: clearly-correct
0-3-8_b0.note: A♭/C is correct because A♭-C-E♭ is a complete major triad with C as the third in the bass, a conventional first inversion. Music21 agrees semantically. Tonal's Cm♯5 is pitch-class equivalent, but it uses rarer augmented-minor vocabulary and respells the observed A♭ as G♯ rather than naming the plain major-triad inversion most musicians would expect.
